4.1.label: Question 4.1
4.1.synopsis: Policy data, actuarial assumptions, model/parameter/random risk, competition, guarantees, management, counterparties (reinsurance/distribution/medical), regulation, customer service, internal audit, physical risks, aggregation, catastrophes, policy wording, non-disclosure, medical science advances
4.1.question: List the main risks facing a health insurer.

Risks facing a health insurer: Inadequate, inaccurate or incomplete policy data.
Data used in the formulation of actuarial assumptions may be inadequate or not applicable to the purpose for which the actuary requires them.

Risks associated with assumptions (eg claim inception, mortality and recovery rates, average claim amounts, investment performance, expenses, expense inflation, withdrawals, volume and mix of new business). These risks can be classified as follows:
– Model risk: the risk that the underlying model is not adequate.
– Parameter risk: the risk that the parameters assumed for the underlying model are incorrect.
– Random fluctuations risk: the risk of unpredictable fluctuations arising from sample error.
Risks that the company may take as a result of competition, eg to reduce premium rates, to offer additional guarantees and options, or to increase commissions.
Risks associated with calculating the cost of guarantees and options.
Management may also take risks that, in the actuaries' view, are unacceptable in order to increase the size of the business or to maximise short-term shareholder earnings.
Counterparties under reinsurance arrangements, eg failure of a reinsurer.
Counterparties in distribution may commit the insurer to new conditions, delay premium or claim payments, become bankrupt or bring the insurer into disrepute.
Counterparties in the provision of medical services, in that they can have influence over claims costs and the client relationship.
Regulation and fiscal developments, eg tax, policy conditions and premium rating.
Customer service shortcomings / reputational risks.
Internal audit failures / fraud.
Physical risks including IT recovery, eg fire, flood, impact, loss of key staff.
Aggregation and concentration of risk, eg as a result of specialisation of product or over concentration of distribution.
Catastrophes such as epidemics and wars.
Policy wording risk, ie claims end up being paid that were not originally intended to be covered.
Non-disclosure / misrepresentation, ie the decision by the policyholder not to share all risk-related information, especially if a moratorium approach is used.
Advances in medical science may increase health insurance claims – in particular, early diagnosis of conditions, eg as a result of national screening programmes.

4.2.label: Question 4.2
4.2.synopsis: Claim inception and termination rates, recoveries, deaths, leniency of criteria, anti-selection, selective withdrawals
4.2.question: Describe the most important risks associated particularly with IP insurance and LTCI.

Risks for IP insurance and LTCI: The main risk for IP insurance and LTCI relates to the claim inception and claim termination rates. Terminations include recoveries and deaths. This risk increases according to the leniency of the criteria used to determine a claim. Associated with this is the risk of anti-selection and the risk of selective withdrawals.

4.3.label: Question 4.3
4.3.synopsis: Rates of diagnosis of critical illness, anti-selection, selective withdrawal
4.3.question: Describe the most important risks associated particularly with CI insurance.

Risks for CI insurance: The main risk for CI insurance is in respect of the rates of diagnosis of critical illness (ie leading to a claim), along with the impact of anti-selection and selective withdrawal.

4.4.label: Question 4.4
4.4.synopsis: Lack of control over benefit payments, GP referrals influence incidence, providers determine claim amounts
4.4.question: Describe the most important risks associated particularly with PMI.

Risks for PMI: The main risk under PMI is the lack of control that insurers have over benefit payments. The GP referrals process largely influences incidence, and providers such as hospitals and specialists often determine claim amounts.

(i).synopsis: Cradle to grave, company ethos, attract and retain staff, reward long-serving employees and directors
(i).question:
A company provides a post-retirement plan for its employees. As part of the retirement benefits, the company offers a private medical expenses plan, where it pays for private medical treatment for all retirees until death.

Suggest the reasons why a company would wish to provide a private medical expenses plan for its retirees.

Reasons for offering a private medical e: This could be offered to provide 'cradle to grave' benefits to employees. [½]
This may align with the company's ethos around benefit provision. [½]
It may also help attract and retain good quality staff for the duration of their working lifetime. [1]
In particular, it may use this to reward long-serving employees and senior staff such as directors. [½]

(ii).synopsis: Guarantee PMI for life, expensive, costs increase with age above inflation, longevity risk, complex to administer, contact details, self-insure risk, HR issues with spouses/dependants, accelerated claims risk
(ii).question:
A company provides a post-retirement plan for its employees. As part of the retirement benefits, the company offers a private medical expenses plan, where it pays for private medical treatment for all retirees until death.

Outline the disadvantages to a company of providing such a plan.

Disadvantages to the company: The company are essentially guaranteeing retirees private medical cover for life. [½]
If they purchase private medical insurance (PMI) this could be expensive, ... [½]
... particularly as the retirees age and the cost of cover increases. [½]
The company could choose to self-insure, however this could also lead to very high costs in the event of a large claim. [½]
Medical costs are likely to increase rapidly over time as they generally increase at a rate in excess of other types of inflation (eg price or salary inflation). [½]
The company will have longevity risk associated with having to pay claims (or insurance premiums) for longer than expected. [½]
The cover may be complex and time consuming to administer. [½]
For example, maintaining contact details for retirees may be complicated. [½]
This will lead to additional costs for the company that will need to be covered. [½]
If the retirement cover just covers the individual that was employed, it could cause issues if spouses or dependants were covered during employment. [½]
There may be a risk of accelerated claims before retirement if spouses or dependants are not covered post retirement. [½]
